content.text: Azure networking services connect virtual machines, applications, databases, and on-premises infrastructure into coherent, secure, and performant systems. They span four functional areas: connectivity (VNets, VPN Gateway, ExpressRoute), load balancing and delivery (Load Balancer, Application Gateway, Front Door), network security (NSGs, Azure Firewall, WAF, DDoS Protection), and DNS and observability (Azure DNS, Network Watcher, Flow Logs). Unlike on-premises networking, Azure's networking services are software-defined: you configure them through APIs, ARM templates, or the portal, and the physical infrastructure is fully managed by Azure.

attrs.alt: Azure networking services grouped into four categories: Connectivity (blue), Load Balancing & Delivery (green), Network Security (amber), DNS & Monitoring (pink)

content.text: A Virtual Network is the foundational networking primitive in Azure. It is a logically isolated network segment in the Azure cloud, defined by one or more IPv4 CIDR blocks (address spaces) and subdivided into subnets. Resources deployed into a VNet (VMs, App Service Environments, AKS nodes, Azure SQL Managed Instances) get private IP addresses from their subnet's range and can communicate with each other without leaving the Azure backbone.

VNets exist within a single Azure region and subscription. They do not span regions — inter-region connectivity requires VNet Peering or Virtual WAN. Resources in different VNets (even in the same subscription and region) cannot communicate by default; you must explicitly enable peering or use a gateway.

# Create a VNet with two subnets
az network vnet create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVNet \
  --address-prefix 10.0.0.0/16 \
  --location eastus

az network vnet subnet create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --name WebSubnet \
  --address-prefix 10.0.1.0/24

az network vnet subnet create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --name DataSubnet \
  --address-prefix 10.0.2.0/24

# Add an additional address space to an existing VNet
az network vnet update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVNet \
  --add addressSpace.addressPrefixes "10.1.0.0/16"

# List all subnets and their address prefixes
az network vnet subnet list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --query "[].{name:name, prefix:addressPrefix, privateEndpoint:privateEndpointNetworkPolicies}" \
  --output table

# Check which resources are deployed in a subnet
az network vnet subnet show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --name WebSubnet \
  --query "ipConfigurations[].id" \
  --output tsv

VNet Peering connects two VNets so that resources in each can communicate using private IP addresses over the Azure backbone, without traversing the public internet. Traffic between peered VNets is not encrypted (the Azure backbone is Microsoft-private infrastructure), and the bandwidth is effectively unlimited — it routes at the same speed as traffic within a single VNet.

Two types of peering exist: regional peering (both VNets in the same region, billed at a lower rate) and global peering (VNets in different regions, billed at a higher rate based on data transfer volume). Peering is non-transitive: if VNet A is peered with VNet B, and VNet B is peered with VNet C, VNet A cannot reach VNet C unless A-C are also peered or a hub-and-spoke Virtual WAN is used.

# Peer two VNets in the same region (bidirectional — requires two peering resources)
VNET_A_ID=$(az network vnet show -g myRG -n vnetA --query id -o tsv)
VNET_B_ID=$(az network vnet show -g myRG -n vnetB --query id -o tsv)

# Create peering from A to B
az network vnet peering create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name AtoB \
  --vnet-name vnetA \
  --remote-vnet "$VNET_B_ID" \
  --allow-vnet-access \
  --allow-forwarded-traffic

# Create peering from B to A
az network vnet peering create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name BtoA \
  --vnet-name vnetB \
  --remote-vnet "$VNET_A_ID" \
  --allow-vnet-access \
  --allow-forwarded-traffic

# Verify peering state (both must be "Connected" for traffic to flow)
az network vnet peering list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vnet-name vnetA \
  --query "[].{name:name, remoteVnet:remoteVirtualNetwork.id, state:peeringState}" \
  --output table
These two services connect on-premises networks to Azure VNets:

encrypted IPsec/IKE tunnels over the public internet. Site-to-Site VPN connects an on-premises VPN device to Azure; Point-to-Site connects individual client machines. Maximum bandwidth is 10 Gbps (VpnGw5 SKU). VPN Gateway has a 15–45 minute deployment time and is the standard solution for branch offices, small organizations, or backup connectivity.

a dedicated private circuit provisioned through a connectivity provider (AT&T, Equinix, BT, etc.) that bypasses the public internet. Provides 50 Mbps to 100 Gbps with predictable latency. Traffic never traverses the internet. Required for financial services, healthcare, and any workload with strict latency or compliance requirements. ExpressRoute circuits take days to weeks to provision.
Not encrypted by default; MACsec optional

# Create a VPN Gateway (Basic SKU for dev/test; VpnGw1 for production)
# First create the GatewaySubnet (name is fixed, must be /27 or larger)
az network vnet subnet create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --name GatewaySubnet \
  --address-prefix 10.0.255.0/27

# Create a public IP for the gateway
az network public-ip create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name vpnGatewayIP \
  --sku Standard \
  --allocation-method Static

# Create the VPN Gateway (takes 20–45 minutes)
az network vnet-gateway create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVPNGateway \
  --location eastus \
  --vnet myVNet \
  --public-ip-address vpnGatewayIP \
  --gateway-type Vpn \
  --vpn-type RouteBased \
  --sku VpnGw1 \
  --no-wait

# Add a Site-to-Site connection to an on-premises VPN device
az network local-gateway create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myOnPremGW \
  --local-address-prefixes 192.168.0.0/16 \
  --gateway-ip-address 203.0.113.10  # on-premises public IP

az network vpn-connection create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name s2sConnection \
  --vnet-gateway1 myVPNGateway \
  --local-gateway2 myOnPremGW \
  --shared-key "PSK_GOES_HERE"

# List VPN connections and their status
az network vpn-connection list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --query "[].{name:name, status:connectionStatus, egress:egressBytesTransferred, ingress:ingressBytesTransferred}" \
  --output table

A Network Security Group is a stateful packet filter applied to a NIC or a subnet. It contains inbound and outbound security rules, each specifying: source (IP, CIDR, service tag, or ASG), destination, protocol (TCP/UDP/ICMP/Any), port range, and action (Allow or Deny). Rules are evaluated in priority order (100 to 4096, lower is higher priority); the first matching rule wins.

Azure provides default rules (DenyAllInbound, AllowAzureLoadBalancerInbound, AllowVNetInBound for inbound; AllowVNetOutBound, AllowInternetOutBound, DenyAllOutBound for outbound) that you can override but cannot delete. NSGs are stateful — allowing inbound TCP:443 automatically allows the return traffic without an explicit outbound rule.

# Create an NSG
az network nsg create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name webNSG \
  --location eastus

# Allow HTTPS inbound from anywhere (priority 100)
az network nsg rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --nsg-name webNSG \
  --name AllowHTTPS \
  --priority 100 \
  --protocol Tcp \
  --direction Inbound \
  --source-address-prefixes "*" \
  --source-port-ranges "*" \
  --destination-address-prefixes "*" \
  --destination-port-ranges 443 \
  --access Allow

# Allow SSH only from a corporate IP range (priority 110)
az network nsg rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --nsg-name webNSG \
  --name AllowSSHCorp \
  --priority 110 \
  --protocol Tcp \
  --direction Inbound \
  --source-address-prefixes "10.10.0.0/16" \
  --destination-port-ranges 22 \
  --access Allow

# Deny all other inbound traffic explicitly (lower priority than the allows)
az network nsg rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --nsg-name webNSG \
  --name DenyAllOtherInbound \
  --priority 4000 \
  --protocol "*" \
  --direction Inbound \
  --source-address-prefixes "*" \
  --destination-port-ranges "*" \
  --access Deny

# Associate the NSG with a subnet
az network vnet subnet update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --name WebSubnet \
  --network-security-group webNSG

# View effective security rules on a VM's NIC (shows both NIC-level and subnet-level NSG rules merged)
az network nic list-effective-nsg \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVMNic \
  --query "effectiveSecurityRules[?direction=='Inbound'].{priority:priority, name:name, access:access, port:destinationPortRange}" \
  --output table
Azure Load Balancer vs Application Gateway vs Front Door
Azure provides three distinct load balancing services at different layers of the network stack. Choosing the wrong one is a common mistake that forces re-architecture later.
5-tuple hash (src IP, dst IP, src port, dst port, protocol)
URL path, hostname, headers, cookies, SSL session
URL path, hostname, latency-based routing, geo-based routing
DNS response based on routing method (latency, priority, geographic, weighted)

# Create an Azure Load Balancer (Standard tier, zone-redundant)
az network lb create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myLB \
  --sku Standard \
  --public-ip-address myLBPublicIP \
  --frontend-ip-name myFrontend \
  --backend-pool-name myBackendPool

# Add a load balancing rule (TCP:80 → backend pool :80)
az network lb rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --lb-name myLB \
  --name httpRule \
  --protocol Tcp \
  --frontend-port 80 \
  --backend-port 80 \
  --frontend-ip-name myFrontend \
  --backend-pool-name myBackendPool \
  --probe-name healthProbe

# Add a health probe
az network lb probe create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --lb-name myLB \
  --name healthProbe \
  --protocol Http \
  --port 80 \
  --path /health

# Create an Application Gateway (WAF_v2 SKU with Web Application Firewall)
az network application-gateway create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAppGateway \
  --location eastus \
  --sku WAF_v2 \
  --capacity 2 \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --subnet AppGWSubnet \
  --public-ip-address myAppGWIP \
  --http-settings-cookie-based-affinity Disabled \
  --frontend-port 443 \
  --http-settings-port 80 \
  --http-settings-protocol Http \
  --routing-rule-type Basic \
  --priority 100

# Create an Azure Front Door profile (Standard tier)
az afd profile create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFrontDoor \
  --sku Standard_AzureFrontDoor

# Add an origin group and origin
az afd origin-group create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFrontDoor \
  --origin-group-name myOriginGroup \
  --probe-request-type GET \
  --probe-protocol Https \
  --probe-interval-in-seconds 30 \
  --sample-size 4 \
  --successful-samples-required 3

az afd origin create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFrontDoor \
  --origin-group-name myOriginGroup \
  --origin-name myOrigin \
  --host-name mywebapp.azurewebsites.net \
  --origin-host-header mywebapp.azurewebsites.net \
  --http-port 80 \
  --https-port 443 \
  --priority 1 \
  --weight 1000

Azure DNS provides authoritative DNS hosting for public domains and private DNS resolution within VNets. The two services are distinct: Public Azure DNS zones are reachable from the internet (hosting your domain's A, CNAME, MX, TXT records); Private Azure DNS zones are reachable only from linked VNets (resolving internal names like myvm.internal.contoso.com).

Azure DNS is not a domain registrar — you must register your domain with any registrar (GoDaddy, Namecheap, etc.) and then delegate name resolution to Azure DNS by pointing the domain's NS records at the Azure name servers assigned to your zone.

# Create a public DNS zone for a domain
az network dns zone create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name contoso.com

# Get the Azure DNS name servers for the zone (add these as NS records at your registrar)
az network dns zone show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name contoso.com \
  --query nameServers \
  --output tsv

# Add an A record (web → 20.10.20.30)
az network dns record-set a add-record \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --zone-name contoso.com \
  --record-set-name www \
  --ipv4-address 20.10.20.30

# Add a CNAME record (api → mywebapp.azurewebsites.net)
az network dns record-set cname set-record \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --zone-name contoso.com \
  --record-set-name api \
  --cname mywebapp.azurewebsites.net

# Add a TXT record (for domain verification / SPF)
az network dns record-set txt add-record \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --zone-name contoso.com \
  --record-set-name "@" \
  --value "v=spf1 include:spf.protection.outlook.com -all"

# Create a Private DNS Zone for internal resolution
az network private-dns zone create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name "internal.contoso.com"

# Link the private zone to a VNet (with auto-registration — VMs get DNS entries automatically)
az network private-dns link vnet create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --zone-name "internal.contoso.com" \
  --name myVNetLink \
  --virtual-network myVNet \
  --registration-enabled true

# Manually add a record to the private zone
az network private-dns record-set a add-record \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --zone-name "internal.contoso.com" \
  --record-set-name db-primary \
  --ipv4-address 10.0.2.10

Network Watcher is the Azure network observability service. It provides per-region tools for diagnosing connectivity issues, capturing traffic, and auditing network configuration. Network Watcher must be enabled per region (it is created automatically when you create a VNet in a new region for most subscription types).
tests connectivity between two endpoints (VM to VM, VM to URL) and shows the path, hops, and any NSG rules blocking traffic.
checks whether a specific packet (src IP, dst IP, port, protocol, direction) would be allowed or denied by the NSGs attached to a VM's NIC and subnet.
capture information about which traffic flows are allowed or denied by an NSG. Flow logs are written to a Storage Account and can be ingested by Log Analytics for traffic analysis.
records raw packets from a VM's NIC to a Storage Account or local file. Supports filters by protocol, IP, and port.
similar to NSG flow logs but scoped to an entire VNet rather than a specific NSG. Provides broader visibility without needing to attach flow logs to every NSG.

# Enable Network Watcher in a region (usually auto-created, but can be explicit)
az network watcher configure \
  --resource-group NetworkWatcherRG \
  --locations eastus \
  --enabled true

# IP flow verify: check if TCP:443 inbound from 203.0.113.5 is allowed on a VM
az network watcher test-ip-flow \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vm myVM \
  --direction Inbound \
  --protocol TCP \
  --local 10.0.1.4:443 \
  --remote 203.0.113.5:12345

# Connection troubleshoot: check if myVM can reach 8.8.8.8 on port 443
az network watcher test-connectivity \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --source-resource $(az vm show -g myRG -n myVM --query id -o tsv) \
  --dest-address 8.8.8.8 \
  --dest-port 443

# Enable NSG flow logs on an NSG (writes to storage account)
az network watcher flow-log create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myFlowLog \
  --nsg webNSG \
  --storage-account $(az storage account show -g myRG -n mystorageaccount --query id -o tsv) \
  --location eastus \
  --retention 30 \
  --log-version 2 \
  --traffic-analytics true \
  --workspace $(az monitor log-analytics workspace show -g myRG -n myLAW --query id -o tsv)

# Run a packet capture on a VM (captures first 500 MB or 180 seconds, whichever comes first)
az network watcher packet-capture create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vm myVM \
  --name myCapture \
  --storage-account mystorageaccount \
  --time-limit 180 \
  --total-bytes-per-session 52428800 \
  --filters '[{"protocol":"TCP","remoteIPAddress":"203.0.113.5","remotePort":"443"}]'

# Show packet capture status
az network watcher packet-capture show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myCapture \
  --query "{status:provisioningState, bytesLeft:totalBytesPerSession}" \
  --output json

Azure Private Link allows you to access Azure PaaS services (Azure Storage, Azure SQL Database, Azure Key Vault, and many others) and partner services via a private IP address inside your VNet, rather than over the public internet. The traffic stays on the Microsoft backbone and never leaves your VNet.

A Private Endpoint is a NIC in your subnet with a private IP that maps to a specific PaaS service resource. When combined with a Private DNS Zone, clients in the VNet resolve the service's public hostname (e.g., mystorageaccount.blob.core.windows.net) to the private IP automatically — no application code changes required.

# Create a Private Endpoint for an Azure Storage account
STORAGE_ID=$(az storage account show -g myRG -n mystorageaccount --query id -o tsv)

az network private-endpoint create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name storagePrivateEndpoint \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --subnet DataSubnet \
  --private-connection-resource-id "$STORAGE_ID" \
  --group-id blob \
  --connection-name storageConnection

# Get the private IP assigned to the endpoint
az network private-endpoint show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name storagePrivateEndpoint \
  --query "customDnsConfigs[0].ipAddresses[0]" \
  --output tsv

# Create a Private DNS Zone for blob storage and link to VNet
az network private-dns zone create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name "privatelink.blob.core.windows.net"

az network private-dns link vnet create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --zone-name "privatelink.blob.core.windows.net" \
  --name blobDNSLink \
  --virtual-network myVNet \
  --registration-enabled false

# Create DNS record for the private endpoint
az network private-endpoint dns-zone-group create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --endpoint-name storagePrivateEndpoint \
  --name storageZoneGroup \
  --private-dns-zone "privatelink.blob.core.windows.net" \
  --zone-name blob

# Disable public network access on the storage account (force all access through private endpoint)
az storage account update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mystorageaccount \
  --public-network-access Disabled

Azure networking services span connectivity, load balancing, security, and observability. The foundational layer is the Virtual Network: every compute resource connects to a VNet subnet and gets a private IP address. On-premises connectivity comes via VPN Gateway (internet, encrypted) or ExpressRoute (private circuit). VNet Peering connects VNets within or across regions over the Microsoft backbone.
Virtual Networks are region-scoped, logically isolated, and subdivided into subnets. Peering is required for cross-VNet communication.
VPN Gateway provides encrypted connectivity to on-premises networks; ExpressRoute provides dedicated private circuits for latency-sensitive or compliance-driven workloads.
Network Security Groups filter traffic at the NIC or subnet level using stateful rules evaluated in priority order.

Azure Load Balancer operates at L4 (TCP/UDP) for regional traffic; Application Gateway operates at L7 (HTTP/S) with URL-based routing and WAF; Azure Front Door operates globally at L7 with geo and latency-based routing.
Azure DNS hosts public authoritative zones and private zones for internal resolution within VNets.
Network Watcher provides IP flow verify, connection troubleshoot, NSG flow logs, and packet capture for diagnosing and auditing network behavior.
Private Link and Private Endpoints bring Azure PaaS services (Storage, SQL, Key Vault) into your VNet via private IPs, keeping traffic off the public internet.
